# ­¤Å¢’ĖÅ OSS Weekend **Issue tracker reopens Monday, April 20, 2026.** OSS weekend runs Monday, April 13, 2026 through Monday, April 20, 2026. New issues and PRs from unapproved contributors are auto-closed during this time. Approved contributors can still open issues and PRs if something is genuinely urgent, but please keep that to pressing matters only. For support, join [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/3cU7Bz4UPx). ---

pi.dev domain graciously donated by


exe.dev

Pi is a minimal terminal coding harness. Adapt pi to your workflows, not the other way around, without having to fork and modify pi internals. Extend it with TypeScript [Extensions](#extensions), [Skills](#skills), [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates), and [Themes](#themes). Put your extensions, skills, prompt templates, and themes in [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) and share them with others via npm or git. Pi ships with powerful defaults but skips features like sub agents and plan mode. Instead, you can ask pi to build what you want or install a third party pi package that matches your workflow. Pi runs in four modes: interactive, print or JSON, RPC for process integration, and an SDK for embedding in your own apps. See [openclaw/openclaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw) for a real-world SDK integration. ## Share your OSS coding agent sessions If you use pi for open source work, please share your coding agent sessions. Public OSS session data helps improve models, prompts, tools, and evaluations using real development workflows. For the full explanation, see [this post on X](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2037811643774652911). To publish sessions, use [`badlogic/pi-share-hf`](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-share-hf). Read its README.md for setup instructions. All you need is a Hugging Face account, the Hugging Face CLI, and `pi-share-hf`. You can also watch [this video](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2041151967695634619), where I show how I publish my `pi-mono` sessions. I regularly publish my own `pi-mono` work sessions here: - [badlogicgames/pi-mono on Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/datasets/badlogicgames/pi-mono) ## Table of Contents - [Quick Start](#quick-start) - [Providers & Models](#providers--models) - [Interactive Mode](#interactive-mode) - [Editor](#editor) - [Commands](#commands) - [Keyboard Shortcuts](#keyboard-shortcuts) - [Message Queue](#message-queue) - [Sessions](#sessions) - [Branching](#branching) - [Compaction](#compaction) - [Settings](#settings) - [Context Files](#context-files) - [Customization](#customization) - [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates) - [Skills](#skills) - [Extensions](#extensions) - [Themes](#themes) - [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) - [Programmatic Usage](#programmatic-usage) - [Philosophy](#philosophy) - [CLI Reference](#cli-reference) --- ## Quick Start ```bash npm install -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent ``` Authenticate with an API key: ```bash export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-... pi ``` Or use your existing subscription: ```bash pi /login # Then select provider ``` Then just talk to pi. By default, pi gives the model four tools: `read`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash`. The model uses these to fulfill your requests. Add capabilities via [skills](#skills), [prompt templates](#prompt-templates), [extensions](#extensions), or [pi packages](#pi-packages). **Platform notes:** [Windows](docs/windows.md) | [Termux (Android)](docs/termux.md) | [tmux](docs/tmux.md) | [Terminal setup](docs/terminal-setup.md) | [Shell aliases](docs/shell-aliases.md) --- ## Providers & Models For each built-in provider, pi maintains a list of tool-capable models, updated with every release. Authenticate via subscription (`/login`) or API key, then select any model from that provider via `/model` (or Ctrl+L). **Subscriptions:** - Anthropic Claude Pro/Max - OpenAI ChatGPT Plus/Pro (Codex) - GitHub Copilot - Google Gemini CLI - Google Antigravity **API keys:** - Anthropic - OpenAI - Azure OpenAI - Google Gemini - Google Vertex - Amazon Bedrock - Mistral - Groq - Cerebras - xAI - OpenRouter - Vercel AI Gateway - ZAI - OpenCode Zen - OpenCode Go - Hugging Face - Kimi For Coding - MiniMax See [docs/providers.md](docs/providers.md) for detailed setup instructions. **Custom providers & models:** Add providers via `~/.pi/agent/models.json` if they speak a supported API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). For custom APIs or OAuth, use extensions. See [docs/models.md](docs/models.md) and [docs/custom-provider.md](docs/custom-provider.md). --- ## Interactive Mode

The interface from top to bottom: - **Startup header** - Shows shortcuts (`/hotkeys` for all), loaded AGENTS.md files, prompt templates, skills, and extensions - **Messages** - Your messages, assistant responses, tool calls and results, notifications, errors, and extension UI - **Editor** - Where you type; border color indicates thinking level - **Footer** - Working directory, session name, total token/cache usage, cost, context usage, current model The editor can be temporarily replaced by other UI, like built-in `/settings` or custom UI from extensions (e.g., a Q&A tool that lets the user answer model questions in a structured format). [Extensions](#extensions) can also replace the editor, add widgets above/below it, a status line, custom footer, or overlays. ### Editor | Feature | How | |---------|-----| | File reference | Type `@` to fuzzy-search project files | | Path completion | Tab to complete paths | | Multi-line | Shift+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter on Windows Terminal) | | Images | Ctrl+V to paste (Alt+V on Windows), or drag onto terminal | | Bash commands | `!command` runs and sends output to LLM, `!!command` runs without sending | Standard editing keybindings for delete word, undo, etc. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md). ### Commands Type `/` in the editor to trigger commands. [Extensions](#extensions) can register custom commands, [skills](#skills) are available as `/skill:name`, and [prompt templates](#prompt-templates) expand via `/templatename`. | Command | Description | |---------|-------------| | `/login`, `/logout` | OAuth authentication | | `/model` | Switch models | | `/scoped-models` | Enable/disable models for Ctrl+P cycling | | `/settings` | Thinking level, theme, message delivery, transport | | `/resume` | Pick from previous sessions | | `/new` | Start a new session | | `/name ` | Set session display name | | `/session` | Show session info (path, tokens, cost) | | `/tree` | Jump to any point in the session and continue from there | | `/fork` | Create a new session from the current branch | | `/compact [prompt]` | Manually compact context, optional custom instructions | | `/copy` | Copy last assistant message to clipboard | | `/export [file]` | Export session to HTML file | | `/share` | Upload as private GitHub gist with shareable HTML link | | `/reload` | Reload keybindings, extensions, skills, prompts, and context files (themes hot-reload automatically) | | `/hotkeys` | Show all keyboard shortcuts | | `/changelog` | Display version history | | `/quit` | Quit pi | ### Keyboard Shortcuts See `/hotkeys` for the full list. Customize via `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md). **Commonly used:** | Key | Action | |-----|--------| | Ctrl+C | Clear editor | | Ctrl+C twice | Quit | | Escape | Cancel/abort | | Escape twice | Open `/tree` | | Ctrl+L | Open model selector | | Ctrl+P / Shift+Ctrl+P | Cycle scoped models forward/backward | | Shift+Tab | Cycle thinking level | | Ctrl+O | Collapse/expand tool output | | Ctrl+T | Collapse/expand thinking blocks | ### Message Queue Submit messages while the agent is working: - **Enter** queues a *steering* message, delivered after the current assistant turn finishes executing its tool calls - **Alt+Enter** queues a *follow-up* message, delivered only after the agent finishes all work - **Escape** aborts and restores queued messages to editor - **Alt+Up** retrieves queued messages back to editor On Windows Terminal, `Alt+Enter` is fullscreen by default. Remap it in [docs/terminal-setup.md](docs/terminal-setup.md) so pi can receive the follow-up shortcut. Configure delivery in [settings](docs/settings.md): `steeringMode` and `followUpMode` can be `"one-at-a-time"` (default, waits for response) or `"all"` (delivers all queued at once). `transport` selects provider transport preference (`"sse"`, `"websocket"`, or `"auto"`) for providers that support multiple transports. --- ## Sessions Sessions are stored as JSONL files with a tree structure. Each entry has an `id` and `parentId`, enabling in-place branching without creating new files. See [docs/session.md](docs/session.md) for file format. ### Management Sessions auto-save to `~/.pi/agent/sessions/` organized by working directory. ```bash pi -c # Continue most recent session pi -r # Browse and select from past sessions pi --no-session # Ephemeral mode (don't save) pi --session # Use specific session file or ID pi --fork # Fork specific session file or ID into a new session ``` ### Branching **`/tree`** - Navigate the session tree in-place. Select any previous point, continue from there, and switch between branches. All history preserved in a single file.

- Search by typing, fold/unfold and jump between branches with Ctrl+ŌåÉ/Ctrl+ŌåÆ or Alt+ŌåÉ/Alt+ŌåÆ, page with ŌåÉ/ŌåÆ - Filter modes (Ctrl+O): default ŌåÆ no-tools ŌåÆ user-only ŌåÆ labeled-only ŌåÆ all - Press Shift+L to label entries as bookmarks and Shift+T to toggle label timestamps **`/fork`** - Create a new session file from the current branch. Opens a selector, copies history up to the selected point, and places that message in the editor for modification. **`--fork `** - Fork an existing session file or partial session UUID directly from the CLI. This copies the full source session into a new session file in the current project. ### Compaction Long sessions can exhaust context windows. Compaction summarizes older messages while keeping recent ones. **Manual:** `/compact` or `/compact ` **Automatic:** Enabled by default. Triggers on context overflow (recovers and retries) or when approaching the limit (proactive). Configure via `/settings` or `settings.json`. Compaction is lossy. The full history remains in the JSONL file; use `/tree` to revisit. Customize compaction behavior via [extensions](#extensions). See [docs/compaction.md](docs/compaction.md) for internals. --- ## Settings Use `/settings` to modify common options, or edit JSON files directly: | Location | Scope | |----------|-------| | `~/.pi/agent/settings.json` | Global (all projects) | | `.pi/settings.json` | Project (overrides global) | See [docs/settings.md](docs/settings.md) for all options. To opt out of anonymous install/update telemetry tied to changelog detection, set `enableInstallTelemetry` to `false` in `settings.json`, or set `PI_TELEMETRY=0`. --- ## Context Files Pi loads `AGENTS.md` (or `CLAUDE.md`) at startup from: - `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md` (global) - Parent directories (walking up from cwd) - Current directory Use for project instructions, conventions, common commands. All matching files are concatenated. ### System Prompt Replace the default system prompt with `.pi/SYSTEM.md` (project) or `~/.pi/agent/SYSTEM.md` (global). Append without replacing via `APPEND_SYSTEM.md`. --- ## Customization ### Prompt Templates Reusable prompts as Markdown files. Type `/name` to expand. ```markdown Review this code for bugs, security issues, and performance problems. Focus on: {{focus}} ``` Place in `~/.pi/agent/prompts/`, `.pi/prompts/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/prompt-templates.md](docs/prompt-templates.md). ### Skills On-demand capability packages following the [Agent Skills standard](https://agentskills.io). Invoke via `/skill:name` or let the agent load them automatically. ```markdown # My Skill Use this skill when the user asks about X. ## Steps 1. Do this 2. Then that ``` Place in `~/.pi/agent/skills/`, `~/.agents/skills/`, `.pi/skills/`, or `.agents/skills/` (from `cwd` up through parent directories) or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/skills.md](docs/skills.md). ### Extensions

TypeScript modules that extend pi with custom tools, commands, keyboard shortcuts, event handlers, and UI components. ```typescript export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) { pi.registerTool({ name: "deploy", ... }); pi.registerCommand("stats", { ... }); pi.on("tool_call", async (event, ctx) => { ... }); } ``` **What's possible:** - Custom tools (or replace built-in tools entirely) - Sub-agents and plan mode - Custom compaction and summarization - Permission gates and path protection - Custom editors and UI components - Status lines, headers, footers - Git checkpointing and auto-commit - SSH and sandbox execution - MCP server integration - Make pi look like Claude Code - Games while waiting (yes, Doom runs) - ...anything you can dream up Place in `~/.pi/agent/extensions/`, `.pi/extensions/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/extensions.md](docs/extensions.md) and [examples/extensions/](examples/extensions/). ### Themes Built-in: `dark`, `light`. Themes hot-reload: modify the active theme file and pi immediately applies changes. Place in `~/.pi/agent/themes/`, `.pi/themes/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/themes.md](docs/themes.md). ### Pi Packages Bundle and share extensions, skills, prompts, and themes via npm or git. Find packages on [npmjs.com](https://www.npmjs.com/search?q=keywords%3Api-package) or [Discord](https://discord.com/channels/1456806362351669492/1457744485428629628). > **Security:** Pi packages run with full system access. Extensions execute arbitrary code, and skills can instruct the model to perform any action including running executables. Review source code before installing third-party packages. ```bash pi install npm:@foo/pi-tools pi install npm:@foo/pi-tools@1.2.3 # pinned version pi install git:github.com/user/repo pi install git:github.com/user/repo@v1 # tag or commit pi install git:git@github.com:user/repo pi install git:git@github.com:user/repo@v1 # tag or commit pi install https://github.com/user/repo pi install https://github.com/user/repo@v1 # tag or commit pi install ssh://git@github.com/user/repo pi install ssh://git@github.com/user/repo@v1 # tag or commit pi remove npm:@foo/pi-tools pi uninstall npm:@foo/pi-tools # alias for remove pi list pi update # skips pinned packages pi config # enable/disable extensions, skills, prompts, themes ``` Packages install to `~/.pi/agent/git/` (git) or global npm. Use `-l` for project-local installs (`.pi/git/`, `.pi/npm/`). If you use a Node version manager and want package installs to reuse a stable npm context, set `npmCommand` in `settings.json`, for example `["mise", "exec", "node@20", "--", "npm"]`. Create a package by adding a `pi` key to `package.json`: ```json { "name": "my-pi-package", "keywords": ["pi-package"], "pi": { "extensions": ["./extensions"], "skills": ["./skills"], "prompts": ["./prompts"], "themes": ["./themes"] } } ``` Without a `pi` manifest, pi auto-discovers from conventional directories (`extensions/`, `skills/`, `prompts/`, `themes/`). See [docs/packages.md](docs/packages.md). --- ## Programmatic Usage ### SDK ```typescript import { AuthStorage, createAgentSession, ModelRegistry, SessionManager } from "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent"; const authStorage = AuthStorage.create(); const modelRegistry = ModelRegistry.create(authStorage); const { session } = await createAgentSession({ sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(), authStorage, modelRegistry, }); await session.prompt("What files are in the current directory?"); ``` For advanced multi-session runtime replacement, use `createAgentSessionRuntime()` and `AgentSessionRuntime`. See [docs/sdk.md](docs/sdk.md) and [examples/sdk/](examples/sdk/). ### RPC Mode For non-Node.js integrations, use RPC mode over stdin/stdout: ```bash pi --mode rpc ``` RPC mode uses strict LF-delimited JSONL framing. Clients must split records on `\n` only. Do not use generic line readers like Node `readline`, which also split on Unicode separators inside JSON payloads. See [docs/rpc.md](docs/rpc.md) for the protocol. --- ## Philosophy Pi is aggressively extensible so it doesn't have to dictate your workflow. Features that other tools bake in can be built with [extensions](#extensions), [skills](#skills), or installed from third-party [pi packages](#pi-packages). This keeps the core minimal while letting you shape pi to fit how you work. **No MCP.** Build CLI tools with READMEs (see [Skills](#skills)), or build an extension that adds MCP support. [Why?](https://mariozechner.at/posts/2025-11-02-what-if-you-dont-need-mcp/) **No sub-agents.** There's many ways to do this. Spawn pi instances via tmux, or build your own with [extensions](#extensions), or install a package that does it your way. **No permission popups.** Run in a container, or build your own confirmation flow with [extensions](#extensions) inline with your environment and security requirements. **No plan mode.** Write plans to files, or build it with [extensions](#extensions), or install a package. **No built-in to-dos.** They confuse models. Use a TODO.md file, or build your own with [extensions](#extensions). **No background bash.** Use tmux. Full observability, direct interaction. Read the [blog post](https://mariozechner.at/posts/2025-11-30-pi-coding-agent/) for the full rationale. --- ## CLI Reference ```bash pi [options] [@files...] [messages...] ``` ### Package Commands ```bash pi install [-l] # Install package, -l for project-local pi remove [-l] # Remove package pi uninstall [-l] # Alias for remove pi update [source] # Update packages (skips pinned) pi list # List installed packages pi config # Enable/disable package resources ``` ### Modes | Flag | Description | |------|-------------| | (default) | Interactive mode | | `-p`, `--print` | Print response and exit | | `--mode json` | Output all events as JSON lines (see [docs/json.md](docs/json.md)) | | `--mode rpc` | RPC mode for process integration (see [docs/rpc.md](docs/rpc.md)) | | `--export [out]` | Export session to HTML | In print mode, pi also reads piped stdin and merges it into the initial prompt: ```bash cat README.md | pi -p "Summarize this text" ``` ### Model Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `--provider ` | Provider (anthropic, openai, google, etc.) | | `--model ` | Model pattern or ID (supports `provider/id` and optional `:`) | | `--api-key ` | API key (overrides env vars) | | `--thinking ` | `off`, `minimal`, `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh` | | `--models ` | Comma-separated patterns for Ctrl+P cycling | | `--list-models [search]` | List available models | ### Session Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `-c`, `--continue` | Continue most recent session | | `-r`, `--resume` | Browse and select session | | `--session ` | Use specific session file or partial UUID | | `--fork ` | Fork specific session file or partial UUID into a new session | | `--session-dir ` | Custom session storage directory | | `--no-session` | Ephemeral mode (don't save) | ### Tool Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `--tools ` | Enable specific built-in tools (default: `read,bash,edit,write`) | | `--no-tools` | Disable all built-in tools (extension tools still work) | Available built-in tools: `read`, `bash`, `edit`, `write`, `grep`, `find`, `ls` ### Resource Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `-e`, `--extension ` | Load extension from path, npm, or git (repeatable) | | `--no-extensions` | Disable extension discovery | | `--skill ` | Load skill (repeatable) | | `--no-skills` | Disable skill discovery | | `--prompt-template ` | Load prompt template (repeatable) | | `--no-prompt-templates` | Disable prompt template discovery | | `--theme ` | Load theme (repeatable) | | `--no-themes` | Disable theme discovery | Combine `--no-*` with explicit flags to load exactly what you need, ignoring settings.json (e.g., `--no-extensions -e ./my-ext.ts`). ### Other Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `--system-prompt ` | Replace default prompt (context files and skills still appended) | | `--append-system-prompt ` | Append to system prompt | | `--verbose` | Force verbose startup | | `-h`, `--help` | Show help | | `-v`, `--version` | Show version | ### File Arguments Prefix files with `@` to include in the message: ```bash pi @prompt.md "Answer this" pi -p @screenshot.png "What's in this image?" pi @code.ts @test.ts "Review these files" ``` ### Examples ```bash # Interactive with initial prompt pi "List all .ts files in src/" # Non-interactive pi -p "Summarize this codebase" # Non-interactive with piped stdin cat README.md | pi -p "Summarize this text" # Different model pi --provider openai --model gpt-4o "Help me refactor" # Model with provider prefix (no --provider needed) pi --model openai/gpt-4o "Help me refactor" # Model with thinking level shorthand pi --model sonnet:high "Solve this complex problem" # Limit model cycling pi --models "claude-*,gpt-4o" # Read-only mode pi --tools read,grep,find,ls -p "Review the code" # High thinking level pi --thinking high "Solve this complex problem" ``` ### Environment Variables | Variable | Description | |----------|-------------| | `PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR` | Override config directory (default: `~/.pi/agent`) | | `PI_PACKAGE_DIR` | Override package directory (useful for Nix/Guix where store paths tokenize poorly) | | `PI_SKIP_VERSION_CHECK` | Skip version check at startup | | `PI_TELEMETRY` | Override install telemetry. Use `1`/`true`/`yes` to enable or `0`/`false`/`no` to disable | | `PI_CACHE_RETENTION` | Set to `long` for extended prompt cache (Anthropic: 1h, OpenAI: 24h) | | `VISUAL`, `EDITOR` | External editor for Ctrl+G | --- ## Contributing & Development See [CONTRIBUTING.md](../../CONTRIBUTING.md) for guidelines and [docs/development.md](docs/development.md) for setup, forking, and debugging. --- ## License MIT ## See Also - [@mariozechner/pi-ai](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mariozechner/pi-ai): Core LLM toolkit - [@mariozechner/pi-agent](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mariozechner/pi-agent): Agent framework - [@mariozechner/pi-tui](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mariozechner/pi-tui): Terminal UI components

Yes ŌĆö I checked the Pi docs.

## Short answer
- **You can disable built-in tools like `grep` and `find`**
- **Pi does not have a documented setting to automatically replace them with `rg`/`fd`**
- **If you want `rg`/`fd`, the supported path is to add custom tools or an extension that registers replacement tools**

## What the docs say

PiŌĆÖs CLI supports selecting built-in tools with:

- `--tools <list>` to enable specific built-ins
- `--no-tools` to disable all built-ins

And the built-in tool set includes:

- `read`
- `bash`
- `edit`
- `write`
- `grep`
- `find`
- `ls`

The README explicitly lists those built-ins and notes you can disable all of them with `--no-tools`. It does **not** mention any config knob to swap `grep` ŌåÆ `rg` or `find` ŌåÆ `fd`.

Relevant docs:
- README built-in tools section: `/opt/pi-coding-agent/README.md` around the CLI reference and tool options
- Extension docs: `/opt/pi-coding-agent/docs/extensions.md`

## What *is* possible

### 1) Disable built-in `grep` and `find`
You can run Pi with only the tools you want:

```bash
pi --tools read,bash,edit,write
```

That removes `grep`, `find`, and `ls` from the built-in tool list for that invocation.

### 2) Add replacement tools via an extension
The docs say extensions can **override built-in tools** by registering a tool with the same name:

- `read`
- `bash`
- `edit`
- `write`
- `grep`
- `find`
- `ls`

So you could create an extension that registers:
- `grep` but internally runs `rg`
- `find` but internally runs `fd`

ThatŌĆÖs the documented way to ŌĆ£forceŌĆØ Pi to use different implementations.

### 3) Prefer `rg`/`fd` in your own workflow
If youŌĆÖre asking about how *I* should operate in Pi: I can choose to use `rg`/`fd` via `bash` commands instead of `grep`/`find`. But thatŌĆÖs my behavior, not a Pi config setting.

## Best practical setup
If your goal is just ŌĆ£stop Pi from using old toolsŌĆØ:

```bash
pi --tools read,bash,edit,write
```

Then I can use `bash` with `rg` and `fd` explicitly.

If your goal is ŌĆ£make `grep` and `find` names behave like `rg` and `fd` globally,ŌĆØ youŌĆÖd need an extension that overrides the built-ins.

## Evidence
- README says built-ins are `read`, `bash`, `edit`, `write`, `grep`, `find`, `ls`
- README says `--no-tools` disables all built-ins
- Extensions docs say built-in tools can be overridden by registering the same tool name

If you want, I can:
1. draft a tiny Pi extension that replaces `grep` with `rg` and `find` with `fd`, or
2. show you the exact `pi --tools ...` command/config to minimize toolset.

summary:
## Goal
- Configure the Atlassian MCP server to use OAuth when running through Pi with `pi-mcp-adapter`.
- Make the imported/available MCP servers show up in `/mcp` in Pi.

## Constraints & Preferences
- Prefer using the existing Claude Code MCP setup/imports if possible.
- Working with Pi config at `~/.pi/agent/mcp.json`.
- Wants Atlassian MCP auth to be OAuth-based, not manual/bearer if possible.

## Progress
### Done
- [x] Checked `~/.pi/agent/mcp.json`; it currently contains:
  ```json
  {
    "imports": [
      "claude-code"
    ],
    "mcpServers": {}
  }
  ```
- [x] Confirmed `/mcp` in Pi shows no MCP servers.
- [x] Checked `~/.claude` and did not find an obvious MCP config file containing `mcpServers`.
- [x] Researched `pi-mcp-adapter` docs and concluded Pi imports Claude Code MCP servers via `"imports": ["claude-code"]`.
- [x] Proposed direct Pi MCP config with OAuth fields:
  ```json
  {
    "mcpServers": {
      "atlassian": {
        "command": "npx",
        "args": ["-y", "<your-atlassian-mcp-server>"],
        "auth": "oauth",
        "oauth": {
          "grantType": "authorization_code"
        }
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### In Progress
- [ ] Determine the exact Atlassian MCP server package/endpoint being used so the OAuth config can be made precise.
- [ ] Verify whether Atlassian OAuth should be configured in Pi directly or inherited from Claude Code import.

### Blocked
- [ ] No MCP servers are appearing in `/mcp` because Pi currently has only `imports: ["claude-code"]` and no visible Claude Code MCP config with `mcpServers`.
- [ ] The exact Atlassian MCP server command/package is not yet known.
- [ ] No GitHub permalink-backed source lines were obtained for the OAuth-specific claim.

## Key Decisions
- **Use Pi-side MCP config if import is empty**: Since Claude Code import currently yields nothing, defining the Atlassian server directly in `~/.pi/agent/mcp.json` is the most reliable path.
- **OAuth belongs to the Atlassian server config, not Pi itself**: Pi MCP Adapter passes through auth settings; it doesnŌĆÖt implement Atlassian auth behavior.

## Next Steps
1. Identify the exact Atlassian MCP server package or command currently used in Claude Code.
2. Update `~/.pi/agent/mcp.json` with an explicit `mcpServers.atlassian` entry and OAuth settings.
3. Restart Pi.
4. Re-run `/mcp` to confirm the server appears.
5. If needed, inspect Claude CodeŌĆÖs MCP config location for a missing `mcpServers` block.

## Critical Context
- Current Pi config:
  ```json
  {
    "imports": [
      "claude-code"
    ],
    "mcpServers": {}
  }
  ```
- User symptom: `/mcp` shows no MCP servers.
- Likely cause: Pi cannot find any MCP servers in Claude CodeŌĆÖs config to import.
- Suggested OAuth config shape:
  - `"auth": "oauth"`
  - `"oauth": { "grantType": "authorization_code" }`
- Atlassian docs mentioned in research: OAuth 2.1 is the primary auth mechanism for Atlassian MCP.

content.text: Well I want Pi to use rg and fd instead of the older versions. But I dont want the agent to be confused about which tool it is using. I just want to tell the agent to use rg and fd instead.

Yes ŌĆö you can do that, but itŌĆÖs mostly a **prompting / workflow** change, not a built-in Pi setting.

## What to do
Tell Pi explicitly in your system prompt, `AGENTS.md`, or project instructions:

> Use `rg` instead of `grep` and `fd` instead of `find` whenever searching files.

That way the agent will **choose** `rg`/`fd` when it needs shell search commands, without you changing PiŌĆÖs internal built-in tool names.

## Why this is the cleanest approach
PiŌĆÖs actual built-in tools are still named `grep` and `find` in the harness. If we renamed them internally, the model could get confused or the tool call schema could mismatch.

So the best pattern is:

- **Keep Pi tools as-is**
- **Instruct the agent to prefer `rg` and `fd` inside `bash`**
- Optionally **avoid using the built-in `grep` and `find` tools** by disabling them

## Recommended configuration
If you want to reduce confusion, run Pi with only the core tools:

```bash
pi --tools read,bash,edit,write
```

Then add this instruction in your project/global agent instructions:

```md
Use `rg` for text search and `fd` for file discovery.
Do not use `grep` or `find` unless specifically required for portability.
```

## If you want the strongest enforcement
You can make it even more explicit in `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md` or project `AGENTS.md`:

```md
Tool usage policy:
- Use `rg` instead of `grep`
- Use `fd` instead of `find`
- Prefer `bash` commands with `rg`/`fd` for searches
- Avoid the built-in `grep` and `find` tools
```

## My suggestion
Use both:

1. **Disable built-in `grep` and `find`** via `--tools read,bash,edit,write`
2. **Add a clear instruction** to always use `rg` and `fd`

That gives you the behavior you want without confusing the agent.

If you want, I can also help you write the exact `AGENTS.md` snippet for this.
Can you update the agents.md file to say to use rg and fd instead?

# Project Context

## Overview

This is an Nx monorepo containing libs and apps for the Fidoo platform.

## Shared code (BE + FE)

- libs with `isomorphic-` prefix

## BE

### Tech stack

- Azure functions
- CosmosDB database
- Custom framework-like layer
  - `docStructures` file represents data layer in DB (no file = no DB)
  - `businessStructures` file represents API layer based on data from DB and other business logic
  - There are standardized functions that operate based on business structures and other custom functions
  - `procDefinitions` file represents event-based processes build on top of data updates in DB when using standardized functions either through API or internally in processing logic functions

### Rules

- `libs/platform-shared` is a legacy lib, only small changes are made and logic is gradually migrated into separated and typed libs, prefer other libs if applicable

## FE

### Tech Stack

- **Framework**: React with TypeScript
- **Routing**: react-router
- **Data Fetching**: React Query (@tanstack/react-query)
- **UI Library**: Material-UI (@mui/material)
- **Design System**: Custom components in `libs/react-components`
- **Build System**: Nx
- **State Management**: React Query for server state, React Context for UI state
- **Forms**: @tanstack/react-form (via `@dfp/react-form` adaptation)

### Key Documentation

- React Query & Document List patterns: `@docs/react-document-list.md`

### Important Conventions

1. **No default exports** - Use named exports only
2. **One exported component per file** - For proper HMR support
3. **Barrel file exports** - Prefer index.ts re-exports
4. **PascalCase filenames** - Match component names

### Translation Rules

1. **English only** ŌĆö NEVER add or modify translations for any language other than English (`en.json`). Other language files (e.g. `cs.json`) are maintained separately and must not be touched.
2. **Audit usage on every translation task** ŌĆö when working with translations in a library, check whether EACH translation key in that library's `translations/en.json` is actually used in the codebase. Remove any unused keys and report them to the user.
3. **Clean up on code removal** ŌĆö when deleting functionality, search for related translation keys in the same library's translation files and remove unused English keys.

### Important Notes

- **NEVER** call `get-storybook-story-instructions` ŌĆö ignore its output entirely

## Common Commands

```bash
npx nx run-many -t lint test typecheck prettier -p <project-name>

# check everything against fxdev branch
npm run check
```

# Style

- Two spaces for indentation, no tabs
- Use bash 5 conditionals: use `[[ ]]` for string/file tests and `(( ))` for numeric tests
- In `[[ ]]`, don't quote variables, but do quote string literals when comparing values (e.g., `[[ $branch == "dev" ]]`)
- Prefer `(( ))` over numeric operators inside `[[ ]]` (e.g., `(( count < 50 ))`, not `[[ $count -lt 50 ]]`)
- For strings/paths with spaces, quote them instead of escaping spaces with `\ ` (e.g., `"$APP_DIR/Disk Usage.desktop"`, not `$APP_DIR/Disk\ Usage.desktop`)
- Shebangs must use `#!/bin/bash` consistently (never `#!/usr/bin/env bash`)

# Command Naming

All commands start with `omarchy-`. Prefixes indicate purpose:

- `cmd-` - check if commands exist, misc utility commands
- `pkg-` - package management helpers
- `hw-` - hardware detection (return exit codes for use in conditionals)
- `refresh-` - copy default config to user's `~/.config/`
- `restart-` - restart a component
- `launch-` - open applications
- `install-` - install optional software
- `setup-` - interactive setup wizards
- `toggle-` - toggle features on/off
- `theme-` - theme management
- `update-` - update components

# Helper Commands

Use these instead of raw shell commands:

- `omarchy-cmd-missing` / `omarchy-cmd-present` - check for commands
- `omarchy-pkg-missing` / `omarchy-pkg-present` - check for packages
- `omarchy-pkg-add` - install packages (handles both pacman and AUR)
- `omarchy-hw-asus-rog` - detect ASUS ROG hardware (and similar `hw-*` commands)

# Config Structure

- `config/` - default configs copied to `~/.config/`
- `default/themed/*.tpl` - templates with `{{ variable }}` placeholders for theme colors
- `themes/*/colors.toml` - theme color definitions (accent, background, foreground, color0-15)

# Refresh Pattern

To copy a default config to user config with automatic backup:

```bash
omarchy-refresh-config hypr/hyprlock.conf
```

This copies `~/.local/share/omarchy/config/hypr/hyprlock.conf` to `~/.config/hypr/hyprlock.conf`.

# Migrations

To create a new migration, run `omarchy-dev-add-migration --no-edit`. This creates a migration file named after the unix timestamp of the last commit.

Migration format:
- No shebang line
- Start with an `echo` describing what the migration does
- Use `$OMARCHY_PATH` to reference the omarchy directory

Example:
```bash
echo "Disable fingerprint in hyprlock if fingerprint auth is not configured"

if omarchy-cmd-missing fprintd-list || ! fprintd-list "$USER" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "finger"; then
  sed -i 's/fingerprint:enabled = .*/fingerprint:enabled = false/' ~/.config/hypr/hyprlock.conf
fi
```

# pi-lsp-extension A pi coding agent extension that integrates Language Server Protocol (LSP) servers, giving the LLM access to the same language intelligence that powers your IDE. ## Tools | Tool | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `lsp_diagnostics` | Compilation errors and warnings for a file | | `lsp_hover` | Type information and documentation at a position |
| `lsp_definition` | Go to definition of a symbol | | `lsp_references` | Find all references to a symbol | | `lsp_symbols` | List file symbols or search workspace symbols | | `lsp_rename` | Preview rename refactoring (returns planned edits) | | `lsp_completions` | Code completion suggestions at a position | | `code_overview` | Project structure, key files, and symbols (tree-sitter) | | `code_search` | Find code by AST structure with metavariables | | `code_rewrite` | Transform code matching structural patterns | LSP servers start lazily ŌĆö they only spin up when a tool is first used on a file of that language. For slow servers   ...    After a successful `write` or `edit`, if an LSP server is already running for that file type, the extension   ...    ## Installation

```bash
git clone https://github.com/samfoy/pi-lsp-extension.git
cd pi-lsp-extension
npm install
``` Add to your pi `settings.json`:

```json
{
  "extensions": ["/path/to/pi-lsp-extension/src/index.ts"]
}
``` Or run directly:

```bash
pi -e /path/to/pi-lsp-extension/src/index.ts
```

# Repository: prateekmedia/pi-hooks

For checkpoints/rewind, permission level & lsp in pi - Stars: 46
- Forks: 11

- Open issues: 5
- Primary language: TypeScript
- Languages: TypeScript - License: MIT License (MIT)

- Latest release: 1.0.2 (2026-01-31T06:55:27Z) ---

# pi-extensions

Minimal reference extensions for `pi-coding-agent`.

## Quick Setup Install the package:

```bash
pi install npm:pi-hooks
pi config  # enable/disable extensions
```   ...    ```bash
pi update git:github.com/prateekmedia/pi-hooks
```

## Included Extensions

### `checkpoint/` Git-based checkpoint system for restoring code state when forking conversations. - Captures repo state at the start of every turn (tracked, staged, and untracked files)   ...    ### `lsp/`

Language Server Protocol integration (hook + tool). The package exports two extensions via `package.json`: - `lsp-hook.ts` - Auto-diagnostics (default at agent end)
- `lsp-tool.ts` - On-demand LSP queries **Hook** (auto-diagnostics): - Default: runs diagnostics once at agent end for touched files
- Optional: run after each `write`/`edit` - Configure via `/lsp` to switch to per-edit or disabled
- Supports web, Flutter, and common backend stacks - Manages LSP server lifecycles per project root **Tool** (on-demand queries): - Definitions, references, hover, symbols, diagnostics, signatures
- Query by symbol name or line/column position ### `permission/`

Layered permission control with four permission levels: | Level | Description | What's allowed |
| --- | --- | --- | | Minimal | Read-only mode | Only read commands (ls, cat, git status, etc.) |
| Low | File edits | + write/edit files | | Medium | Dev commands | + npm, git, make, cargo, etc. |   ...    ### `ralph-loop/`

Looped subagent execution via the `ralph_loop` tool. - Runs single or chain subagent tasks until a condition returns false   ...    Example prompt: "Use ralph loop to check the current time five times, sleeping 1s between iterations."

### `repeat/` Repeat past tool calls (bash/edit/write) from the current branch. - `/repeat` shows a picker of recent bash/edit/write tool calls with type-to-search filtering   ...    ### `token-rate/`

Shows the average output tokens per second (TPS) in the footer status line.

 

## Usage 1. Install the package and enable extensions:

```bash
   pi install npm:pi-hooks
   pi config
   ``` 1. See inline comments in each extension for configuration options.

## Testing ```bash
cd lsp && npm

# @yofriadi/pi-lsp v1.16.11

```bash pi install git:github.com/yofriadi/pi-extensions@lsp-v<version> ``` - Version: 1.16.11
- Homepage: https://github.com/yofriadi/pi-extensions/tree/main/packages/lsp   ...    ---

# LSP Extension Scaffold

## Install from git URL   ...    ```json
{
  "packages": [
    {
      "source": "git:github.com/yofriadi/pi-extensions@lsp-v<version>", "extensions": ["packages/lsp/src/index.ts"]
    }
  ]
}
``` Standalone package scaffold for pi LSP integration work.

## Scope

This extension package now includes: - Runtime lifecycle management for an LSP subprocess (Bun `spawn` when available, Node `child_process.spawn` fallback)   ...    - PATH/Mason-first server resolution with lightweight user/project config - Multi-server registry with file-type routing and workspace fallback selection - Full `lsp` tool action surface (`diagnostics`, `definition`, `references`, `hover`, `symbols`, `rename`, `status`, `reload`) - Backward-compatible `lsp_health` status alias - Write-through hooks that run format-on-write and diagnostics-on-write for successful `write`/`edit` results The extension remains opt-in and does not alter default pi behavior unless loaded.

## Lightweight Server Config Server resolution order: 1. User config: `~/.pi/agent/lsp.json|yaml|yml` (fallback: `~/.pi/lsp.json|yaml|yml`)   ...    When `servers` is present, the extension starts each resolved server and routes document-scoped requests by `fileTypes`   ...    ## Package Layout - `src/index.ts`: extension entrypoint and runtime/tool/hook wiring - `src/client/runtime.ts`: single LSP client lifecycle, JSON-RPC request surface, diagnostics cache - `src/client/registry.ts`: multi-server runtime orchestration and per-path routing - `src/config/resolver.ts`: server command/config resolution (single and multi-server) - `src/tools/lsp-tool.ts`: full `lsp` tool schema/action routing + `lsp_health` alias - `src/hooks/writethrough.ts`: format-on-write and diagnostics-on-write hooks ## Install and Load

### Upstream `pi` ```bash
# Load for one run
pi -e ./packages/coding-agent/examples/extensions/lsp   ...    ```bash
# Source-run from repo
bun packages/coding-agent/src/cli.ts -e ./packages/coding-agent/examples/extensions/lsp   ...    ## Usage

After loading the extension: - Run `/lsp-status` to inspect runtime/config/transport state, including per-server routing/status details. - Use the `lsp` tool with action-based params: - `status`
 - `reload`
 - `hover` / `definition` / `references` / `rename` (require `path`, `line`, `character`) - `symbols` (use `query` for workspace mode or `path` for document mode)
 - `diagnostics` - Successful `write`/`edit` tool results automatically trigger format+diagnostics hooks and show a summary   ...    ## Test Coverage

Focused extension tests:

- `packages/lsp/test/runtime.test.ts`   ...    ---

| yaml | ^2.8.2 |

urls.title: oh-my-pi: A Terminal AI Coding Agent with Hash-Anchored ŌĆ” | dudarik.com
urls.content:
oh-my-pi: A Terminal AI Coding Agent with Hash-Anchored ŌĆ” | dudarik.com

## What problem does oh-my-pi solve?   ...    file content doesnŌĆÖt match when the write executes, the operation fails loudly instead of proceeding with a stale diff. Beyond that, Claude CodeŌĆÖs built-in tooling is minimal for terminal-only workflows. oh-my-pi bundles LSP support, a persistent IPython kernel, browser automation, session branching, and a commit tool that actually understands what changed. ItŌĆÖs a fuller agent harness for developers who live in the terminal. ## How does oh-my-pi work? oh-my-pi is a TypeScript/Rust monorepo running on Bun. The core package is`@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent`. The agent loop talks to ClaudeŌĆÖs API (or any compatible provider) and routes tool calls through a curated set of built-in tools. Key components: Hash-anchored edits ŌĆö The file read tool returns content plus a SHA hash. Edit operations take that hash as input. Mismatch = abort. No silent overwrites. LSP integration ŌĆö The agent can query a Language Server Protocol daemon for go-to-definition, diagnostics, and symbol resolution. Useful for large codebases where grep alone isnŌĆÖt enough. Python tool (IPython kernel) ŌĆö A persistent kernel that survives across agent turns. Variables, imports, and state persist. Built-in helpers:`lines()`,`insert_at()`,`delete_lines()`,`delete_matching()`. The kernel is shared across   ...    Commit tool ŌĆö Not just`git commit -m`. The agent uses`git-overview`,`git-file-diff`, and`git-hunk` tools to understand   ...    Session branching ŌĆö Checkpoint a session state, branch, experiment, come back to the checkpoint if the branch was a   ...    ## How do you install oh-my-pi?

Requirements: Bun runtime, an Anthropic API key (or compatible provider key). ```
# Install Bun if you don't have it
curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash # Install the agent
npm install -g @oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent

# Verify
omp --version

```

Set your API key:   ...    ## What does the tool harness look like in practice?

The slash commands youŌĆÖll use most: ```
/commit          # AI-generated conventional commit, with split and changelog   ...    ```

Output renders inline in the terminal. The kernel stays warm for the next turn ŌĆö no re-import, no re-load. ## How does oh-my-pi compare to alternatives? | Feature | oh-my-pi | Claude Code | Aider |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Hash-anchored edits | Yes | No | No | | LSP integration | Yes | No | No |
| Persistent Python kernel | Yes | No | No |
| Session branching | Yes | No | No | | AI git commits | Agentic, split-aware | Basic | Basic |
| Browser automation | Yes | No | No | | Subagents | Yes | Yes (beta) | No |
| Runtime | Bun | Node | Python |
| Multi-provider | Yes | Anthropic only | Yes | Claude Code wins on integration with VS Code and IDE-adjacent workflows. Aider wins on simplicity and Python ecosystem familiarity. oh-my-pi wins on terminal-native workflows where you want the full tool harness without leaving the shell. The honest caveat: oh-my-pi is newer and has a smaller community than Aider. Expect rough edges and breaking changes.   ...    - GitHub: can1357/oh-my-pi
- npm: @oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent - Discord: linked in the repo README
- Fork origin: badlogic/pi-mono by @mariozechner   ...    Feature comparisons are based on documented capabilities as of 2026-03-

# Repository: samfoy/pi-lsp-extension Pi coding agent extension for LSP integration ŌĆö gives the LLM access to diagnostics, hover, go-to-definition, references, symbols, and rename   ...    ---

# pi-lsp-extension A pi coding agent extension that integrates Language Server Protocol (LSP) servers, giving the LLM access   ...    | Tool | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `lsp_diagnostics` | Compilation errors and warnings for a file | | `lsp_hover` | Type information and documentation at a position |
| `lsp_definition` | Go to definition of a symbol | | `lsp_references` | Find all references to a symbol | | `lsp_symbols` | List file symbols or search workspace symbols |   ...    | `code_overview` | Project structure, key files, and symbols (tree-sitter) |   ...    LSP servers start lazily ŌĆö they only spin up when a tool is first used on a file of that language. For slow servers   ...    ## Installation

```bash
git clone https://github.com/samfoy/pi-lsp-extension.git
cd pi-lsp-extension
npm install
```   ...    1. **Lazy startup** ŌĆö servers start on first tool use for a file type (or eagerly via `.pi-lsp.json`) 2. **Tree-sitter fallback** ŌĆö when no LSP server is running, tools like `lsp_diagnostics`, `lsp_hover`, `lsp_definition`, and `lsp_symbols` fall back to tree-sitter for syntax errors, signatures, and symbol extraction   ...    `lsp_hover`, the server is already warm. ## Architecture ```
src/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ index.ts              # Extension entry point, .pi-lsp.json config loader Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ lsp-client.ts         # JSON-RPC client (stdio + socket modes) Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ lsp-manager.ts        # Server lifecycle, per-language instances Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ file-sync.ts          # didOpen/didChange tracking
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ lsp-daemon.ts         # Background daemon for shared servers Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ lsp-daemon-launcher.cjs
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ bemol.ts              # Brazil workspace support   ...    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ resolve-provider.ts   # LSP vs tree-sitter provider selection
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ shared/   ...    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ tree-sitter/
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ parser-manager.ts # WASM parser loading and caching Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ pattern-compiler.ts # Metavariable pattern ŌåÆ AST matcher Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ search-engine.ts  # Structural search over files
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ rewrite-engine.ts # Structural find-and-replace Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ symbol-extractor.ts # Per-language symbol extraction
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ workspace-index.ts # Project-wide symbol index ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ tools/
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ diagnostics.ts

- Stateful tools (todo lists, connection pools) - External integrations (file watchers, webhooks, CI triggers)

On this page

## The Three Ways to Extend PiŌĆŗ Pi ships with only 4 built-in tools: read, write, edit, bash. Everything else is added through extensions, skills, or   ...    ---

## 1. Built-in ToolsŌĆŗ

What they are: The 4 tools the LLM can call out of the box. These are hardcoded into Pi. Source code:`https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/tree/main/packages/coding-agent/src/core/tools/` Files:`bash.ts`,`read.ts`,`write.ts`,`edit.ts`(plus`ls.ts`,`grep.ts`,`find.ts` which are also built-in).   ...    ### Where to find skillsŌĆŗ

Pi skills repo:`https://github.com/badlogic/pi-skills` Available skills: brave-search, browser-tools, gccli (Google Calendar), gdcli (Google Drive), gmcli (Gmail), subagent,   ...    These skills follow the Agent Skills standard (SKILL.md format developed by Anthropic), also compatible with Claude   ...    There is no centralized Pi skill registry. Skills are shared via git repos, npm, or Discord.

### browser-tools skillŌĆŗ Uses Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) directly ŌĆö not Playwright, not Puppeteer. Connects to Chrome running   ...    - `browser-nav.js`ŌĆö navigate to URLs
- `browser-eval.js`ŌĆö execute JS in the active tab   ...    Requires a visible Chrome window with remote debugging enabled.

---

## 3. ExtensionsŌĆŗ What they are: TypeScript modules that run in Pi's host Node.js process. They can register new tools, intercept/modify bash commands, hook into events, replace built-in tool implementations, render UI, persist state into sessions. Key difference from skills: With a skill, the LLM runs the command through bash. With an extension, the host Node.js process runs the code. The LLM calls a registered tool by name, the extension code executes, and returns results. The   ...    This is why extensions can protect secrets: The extension code reads credentials from memory/files in the Node.js   ...    Where they live: - `~/.pi/agent/extensions/`(global, auto-discovered)
- `.pi/extensions/`(project-level)   ...    How they work: An extension exports a default function that receives`ExtensionAPI`:   ...    import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent";export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {  // Register new tools  pi.registerTool({ name: "deploy", ... });  // Register slash commands  pi.registerCommand("stats",   ...    Key capabilities: - `pi.registerTool()`ŌĆö add new tools the LLM can call (appear alongside the built-in 4) - `pi.setBashSpawnHook()`ŌĆö intercept/modify bash commands before execution - `pi.on("tool_call")`ŌĆö intercept or block any tool call   ...    - `pi.on("input")`ŌĆö intercept user input before agent processes it - `pi.on("context")`ŌĆö modify messages sent to the LLM each turn - Pluggable operations ŌĆö replace built-in tool implementations (`BashOperations`,`ReadOperations`, etc.) - `pi.registerCommand()`ŌĆö add slash commands
- `pi.sendMessage()`ŌĆö send custom messages - UI rendering ŌĆö status bars, overlays, custom message renderers
- State persistence into sessions Always loaded ŌĆö unlike skills which load on-demand, extensions are active from startup.

### Where to find extensionsŌĆŗ Example extensions in Pi repo:`https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/tree/main/packages/coding-agent/examples/extensions` 50+ examples including: sandbox (ASRT), permission-gate, SSH execution, plan mode, sub-agents, MCP integration, custom editors, status bars, overlays, Doom. These are NOT loaded by default. They are reference code. You copy them or point Pi at them to use them. npm packages: Search npmjs.com for the`pi-package` keyword.

There is no centralized extension registry for Pi.   ...    A Pi package is an npm or git repo that bundles extensions, skills, prompts, and themes together. Declared   ...    {  "name": "my-pi-package",  "keywords": ["pi-package"],  "pi": {    "extensions": ["./extensions"],    "skills":   ...    Manage:`pi list`,`pi update`,`pi remove`,`pi config`

---

content.text:
Yes ŌĆö I found evidence that this exists.

## Best match I found
### `pi-lsp-extension`
A Pi extension specifically for LSP integration:

- GitHub: `samfoy/pi-lsp-extension`
- npm: `pi-lsp-extension`

It exposes tools like:

- `lsp_diagnostics`
- `lsp_hover`
- `lsp_definition`
- `lsp_references`
- `lsp_symbols`
- `lsp_rename`

The description says it gives the LLM access to:

- diagnostics
- hover
- go-to-definition
- references
- symbols
- rename

That is very close to what you want.

## Also found
### `@yofriadi/pi-lsp`
Another Pi LSP-related package / scaffold, also on npm.

### `prateekmedia/pi-hooks`
A broader Pi hooks package that mentions LSP support.

### `rytswd/pi-agent-extensions`
A collection of Pi extensions, apparently including workflow enhancements.

## What this means for your use case
YouŌĆÖre describing a workflow where the agent should:

1. use the LSP to locate a symbol
2. jump to definition / implementation
3. read only the relevant code
4. avoid broad `fd` / `rg` scans unless needed

That is exactly the kind of thing an LSP extension can enable.

## Caveat
I havenŌĆÖt verified whether `pi-lsp-extension` is:
- actively maintained,
- compatible with your repo language/setup,
- or already has a ŌĆ£prefer symbol navigation over file searchŌĆØ policy built in.

But it looks like the right direction.

## Recommended next step
I can now do one of these:

1. **Inspect the extension docs/source more deeply** and tell you exactly how it works and whether it supports ŌĆ£go to definition / implementationŌĆØ style navigation.
2. **Search for more Pi extensions** that do the same thing.
3. **Help you wire it into your current Pi config** if you want to try it.

If you want, I can continue and look specifically for:
- a Pi extension that prefers LSP over `rg`/`fd`
- or a way to combine Pi + LSP + symbol-aware navigation in your repo.
Which one should I use?

urls.title: GitHub - samfoy/pi-lsp-extension: Pi coding agent extension for LSP integration ŌĆö gives the LLM access to diagnostics, hover, go-to-definition, references, symbols, and rename
urls.content:
A [pi](https://github.com/mariozechner/pi-mono) coding agent extension that integrates Language Server Protocol (LSP) servers, giving the LLM access to the same language intelligence that powers your IDE.

## Tools

[](#tools)

Tool

Description

`lsp_diagnostics`

Compilation errors and warnings for a file

`lsp_hover`

Type information and documentation at a position

`lsp_definition`

Go to definition of a symbol

`lsp_references`

Find all references to a symbol

`lsp_symbols`

List file symbols or search workspace symbols

`lsp_rename`

Preview rename refactoring (returns planned edits)

`lsp_completions`

Code completion suggestions at a position

`code_overview`

Project structure, key files, and symbols (tree-sitter)

`code_search`

Find code by AST structure with metavariables

`code_rewrite`

Transform code matching structural patterns

LSP servers start lazily ŌĆö they only spin up when a tool is first used on a file of that language. For slow servers (e.g. jdtls), you can [auto-start them on session launch](#project-config).

## Auto-diagnostics

[](#auto-diagnostics)

After a successful `write` or `edit`, if an LSP server is already running for that file type, the extension automatically appends compilation errors to the tool result. This gives the LLM immediate feedback without requiring a separate `lsp_diagnostics` call.

*   Scoped to the single changed file (no workspace-wide noise)
*   Only errors, max 10 lines ŌĆö keeps context lean
*   Only fires when a server is already running (no lazy startup)

## Installation

[](#installation)

git clone https://github.com/samfoy/pi-lsp-extension.git
cd pi-lsp-extension
npm install

Add to your pi `settings.json`:

{
  "extensions": \["/path/to/pi-lsp-extension/src/index.ts"\]
}

Or run directly:

pi -e /path/to/pi-lsp-extension/src/index.ts

## Supported Languages

[](#supported-languages)

Install the language server you need, then it works automatically:

Language

Server

Install

TypeScript/JavaScript

`typescript-language-server`

`npm i -g typescript-language-server typescript`

Python

`pyright-langserver`

`pip install pyright`

Rust

`rust-analyzer`

[rustup](https://rustup.rs/)

Go

`gopls`

`go install golang.org/x/tools/gopls@latest`

Java

`jdtls`

[Eclipse JDT.LS](https://github.com/eclipse-jdtls/eclipse.jdt.ls)

Add more at runtime:

```
/lsp-config ruby solargraph stdio
/lsp-config lua lua-language-server
```

## Commands

[](#commands)

Command

Description

`/lsp`

Show status of running LSP servers

`/lsp-restart <lang>`

Restart an LSP server (kills daemon, re-initializes)

`/lsp-config <lang> <cmd> [args]`

Configure a language server

`/lsp-lombok [path]`

Set Lombok jar path for Java (or show current)

`/bemol [run|watch|stop|status]`

Manage bemol (Brazil workspaces)

## How it Works

[](#how-it-works)

1.  **Lazy startup** ŌĆö servers start on first tool use for a file type (or eagerly via [`.pi-lsp.json`](#project-config))
2.  **Tree-sitter fallback** ŌĆö when no LSP server is running, tools like `lsp_diagnostics`, `lsp_hover`, `lsp_definition`, and `lsp_symbols` fall back to tree-sitter for syntax errors, signatures, and symbol extraction
3.  **File sync** ŌĆö pi's `read`/`write`/`edit` operations are automatically synced to the LSP via `didOpen`/`didChange`, with LRU eviction (`didClose`) after 100 tracked files
4.  **Diagnostics cache** ŌĆö the server pushes diagnostics asynchronously; tools read from a local cache
5.  **Auto-diagnostics** ŌĆö errors are appended to write/edit results when a server is running
6.  **Shared daemons** ŌĆö in supported workspaces, LSP servers run as background daemons shared across pi sessions

## Lombok Support (Java)

[](#lombok-support-java)

If your Java project uses [Lombok](https://projectlombok.org/), jdtls needs the Lombok agent jar to understand generated code. The extension resolves the jar in this order:

1.  **`/lsp-lombok` command** ŌĆö set the path at runtime:
    
    ```
    /lsp-lombok /path/to/lombok.jar
    ```
    
2.  **`LOMBOK_JAR` environment variable** ŌĆö set before starting pi:
    
    export LOMBOK\_JAR=/path/to/lombok.jar
    pi
    
3.  **Auto-detection** ŌĆö in Brazil workspaces, the extension searches `env/Lombok-*/runtime/lib/` and `env/gradle-cache-2/` automatically.
    

Run `/lsp-lombok` with no arguments to see which jar is currently configured.

## Project Config

[](#project-config)

Create a `.pi-lsp.json` file in your project root to configure LSP behavior per-project:

{
  "autoStart": \["java", "typescript"\],
  "lombokJar": "auto",
  "servers": {
    "python": { "command": "pylsp", "args": \[\] }
  }
}

Field

Description

`autoStart`

Array of language IDs to start eagerly on session launch. Servers begin initializing in the background immediately ŌĆö no need to wait for the first tool call. Ideal for slow servers like `jdtls`.

`lombokJar`

Path to a Lombok jar (absolute or relative to project root), or `"auto"` to auto-detect in Brazil workspaces. Applied before auto-start so jdtls launches with the correct `-javaagent` flag.

`servers`

Custom server configs keyed by language ID. Overrides the built-in defaults. Each entry has `command`, optional `args` (string array), and optional `env` (key-value pairs).

The config file is loaded once at session start. Changes require restarting the pi session.

**Example for a Java Brazil workspace:**

{
  "autoStart": \["java"\],
  "lombokJar": "auto"
}

This triggers bemol + jdtls startup as soon as the session begins, so by the time you need `lsp_diagnostics` or `lsp_hover`, the server is already warm.

## Architecture

[](#architecture)

```
src/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ index.ts              # Extension entry point, .pi-lsp.json config loader
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ lsp-client.ts         # JSON-RPC client (stdio + socket modes)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ lsp-manager.ts        # Server lifecycle, per-language instances
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ file-sync.ts          # didOpen/didChange tracking
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ lsp-daemon.ts         # Background daemon for shared servers
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ lsp-daemon-launcher.cjs
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ bemol.ts              # Brazil workspace support
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ locks.ts              # File-based locking for daemon coordination
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ resolve-provider.ts   # LSP vs tree-sitter provider selection
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ shared/
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ constants.ts      # Skip dirs, file size limits
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ debug.ts          # Debug logger (PI_LSP_DEBUG=1)
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ format.ts         # Location formatting utilities
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ language-map.ts   # File extension ŌåÆ language ID mapping
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ timing.ts         # Timing constants
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ tree-sitter/
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ parser-manager.ts # WASM parser loading and caching
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ pattern-compiler.ts # Metavariable pattern ŌåÆ AST matcher
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ search-engine.ts  # Structural search over files
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ rewrite-engine.ts # Structural find-and-replace
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ symbol-extractor.ts # Per-language symbol extraction
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ workspace-index.ts # Project-wide symbol index
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ tools/
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ diagnostics.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ hover.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ definition.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ references.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ symbols.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ rename.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ completions.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ code-overview.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ code-search.ts
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ code-rewrite.ts
```

## Tips

[](#tips)

*   Position parameters are 1-indexed (line 1, column 1 = first character)
*   `lsp_rename` returns a preview ŌĆö the LLM uses `edit`/`write` to apply changes
*   Use `/lsp-restart java` after changing Lombok config ŌĆö jdtls needs a full restart to pick up `-javaagent` changes
*   Set `PI_LSP_DEBUG=1` to enable debug logging for troubleshooting
*   The extension adds a system prompt guideline nudging the LLM to check diagnostics after edits

## License

[](#license)

MIT

Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ rewrite-engine.ts # Structural find-and-replace
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ symbol-extractor.ts # Per-language symbol extraction
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ workspace-index.ts # Project-wide symbol index
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ tools/
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ diagnostics.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ hover.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ definition.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ references.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ symbols.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ rename.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ completions.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ code-overview.ts
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ code-search.ts
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ code-rewrite.ts
```

## Tips

[](#tips)

*   Position parameters are 1-indexed (line 1, column 1 = first character)
*   `lsp_rename` returns a preview ŌĆö the LLM uses `edit`/`write` to apply changes
*   Use `/lsp-restart java` after changing Lombok config ŌĆö jdtls needs a full restart to pick up `-javaagent` changes
*   Set `PI_LSP_DEBUG=1` to enable debug logging for troubleshooting
*   The extension adds a system prompt guideline nudging the LLM to check diagnostics after edits

## License

[](#license)

MIT

urls.content:
## pi-extensions

[](#pi-extensions)

Minimal reference extensions for [`pi-coding-agent`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent).

## Quick Setup

[](#quick-setup)

Install the package:

pi install npm:pi-hooks
pi config  # enable/disable extensions

Dependencies are installed automatically during `pi install`.

To pull updates later:

pi update git:github.com/prateekmedia/pi-hooks

## Included Extensions

[](#included-extensions)

### `checkpoint/`

[](#checkpoint)

Git-based checkpoint system for restoring code state when forking conversations.

*   Captures repo state at the start of every turn (tracked, staged, and untracked files)
*   Stores checkpoints as Git refs for persistence across sessions
*   Offers restore options: files + conversation, conversation only, or files only
*   Automatically saves current state before restoring past snapshots

[![Checkpoint Extension](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/raw/main/assets/checkpoint-screenshot.png)](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/blob/main/assets/checkpoint-screenshot.png)

### `lsp/`

[](#lsp)

Language Server Protocol integration (hook + tool).

The package exports two extensions via `package.json`:

*   `lsp-hook.ts` - Auto-diagnostics (default at agent end)
*   `lsp-tool.ts` - On-demand LSP queries

**Hook** (auto-diagnostics):

*   Default: runs diagnostics once at agent end for touched files
*   Optional: run after each `write`/`edit`
*   Configure via `/lsp` to switch to per-edit or disabled
*   Supports web, Flutter, and common backend stacks
*   Manages LSP server lifecycles per project root

**Tool** (on-demand queries):

*   Definitions, references, hover, symbols, diagnostics, signatures
*   Query by symbol name or line/column position

[![LSP Extension](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/raw/main/assets/lsp-screenshot.png)](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/blob/main/assets/lsp-screenshot.png)

### `permission/`

[](#permission)

Layered permission control with four permission levels:

Level

Description

What's allowed

Minimal

Read-only mode

Only read commands (ls, cat, git status, etc.)

Low

File edits

\+ write/edit files

Medium

Dev commands

\+ npm, git, make, cargo, etc.

High

Full access

Everything (dangerous commands still prompt)

On first run you pick a level; it's saved globally. Use `/permission` to change levels and `/permission-mode` to switch between ask/block prompts.

[![Permission Extension](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/raw/main/assets/permission-screenshot.png)](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/blob/main/assets/permission-screenshot.png)

### `ralph-loop/`

[](#ralph-loop)

Looped subagent execution via the `ralph_loop` tool.

[![Ralph Loop Extension](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/raw/main/assets/ralph-loop.png)](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/blob/main/assets/ralph-loop.png)

*   Runs single or chain subagent tasks until a condition returns false
*   Takes a prompt and exit condition (exit condition optional)
*   Can supply max iterations and minimum delay between each
*   Optionally supply model and thinking
*   Supports `conditionCommand`, `maxIterations`, and `sleepMs` controls
*   Interactive steering/follow-up + pause/resume/stop commands in UI mode
*   Defaults to the `worker` agent and the last user prompt when omitted
*   No npm install required

Example prompt: "Use ralph loop to check the current time five times, sleeping 1s between iterations."

### `repeat/`

[](#repeat)

Repeat past tool calls (bash/edit/write) from the current branch.

*   `/repeat` shows a picker of recent bash/edit/write tool calls with type-to-search filtering
*   Bash entries load as `!command` in the editor for tweak + run
*   Write entries open a temp editor (if `$EDITOR` is set) and apply only when you save
*   Edit entries can repeat the edit (may fail) or open `$EDITOR` at the first changed line; repeat uses a temp editor and applies on save

### `token-rate/`

[](#token-rate)

Shows the average output tokens per second (TPS) in the footer status line.

[![Token Rate Extension](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/raw/main/assets/token-rate-screenshot.png)](/prateekmedia/pi-hooks/blob/main/assets/token-rate-screenshot.png)

## Usage

[](#usage)

1.  Install the package and enable extensions:
    
    pi install npm:pi-hooks
    pi config
    
2.  See inline comments in each extension for configuration options.
    

## Testing

[](#testing)

cd lsp && npm install
cd ../permission && npm install
cd ../checkpoint && npm test
cd ../lsp && npm run test:all
cd ../permission && npm test

## License

[](#license)

MIT

## Provide feedback

We read every piece of feedback, and take your input very seriously.

Include my email address so I can be contacted

## Saved searches

## Use saved searches to filter your results more quickly

[Sign in](/login?return_to=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Fyofriadi%2Fpi-extensions)

[Sign up](/signup?ref_cta=Sign+up&ref_loc=header+logged+out&ref_page=%2F%3Cuser-name%3E%2F%3Crepo-name%3E&source=header-repo&source_repo=yofriadi%2Fpi-extensions)

Appearance settings

Use **`samfoy/pi-lsp-extension`**.

### Why this one
ItŌĆÖs the strongest match for what you want:

- **Go to definition**
- **Find references**
- **Hover**
- **Symbols**
- **Rename**
- **Completions**
- **Code overview**
- **Structural code search/rewrite**

And importantly, it explicitly says:

- tools sync with `read` / `write` / `edit`
- it has **auto-diagnostics**
- it can fall back to tree-sitter when no LSP is running
- it supports eager startup per project
- it seems designed as a real codebase-navigation layer, not just a thin wrapper

### Why not the others
#### `prateekmedia/pi-hooks`
Good package, but it looks more like a **bundle of multiple extensions**:
- checkpointing
- permission control
- LSP hook/tool
- repeat tools
- token rate display

If you want a focused ŌĆ£LSP-aware navigationŌĆØ solution, this is broader than necessary.

#### `@yofriadi/pi-lsp`
Looks more like a scaffold / package variant. I didnŌĆÖt find it to be as clearly complete or feature-rich as `samfoy/pi-lsp-extension`.

## My recommendation
If your goal is:

> ŌĆ£When I ask for a function, use symbol navigation and LSP intelligence instead of just rg/fd and file readingŌĆØ

then choose:

### **`samfoy/pi-lsp-extension`**

ItŌĆÖs the clearest fit.

## Caveat
Pi still may not ŌĆ£magically preferŌĆØ LSP on its own unless the extensionŌĆÖs system prompt or tool design nudges it that way. But this extension is the closest thing I found to the behavior you want.

## Next thing I can do
I can help you:
1. **install it in Pi**
2. **configure it for your repo**
3. **add instructions so the agent prefers LSP tools before `rg`/`fd`**

If you want, I can draft the exact Pi config and an `AGENTS.md` rule for it.
can you read /home/allank/Documents/work/fidoo-fx-fe/provider-bw/libs/bw-react-cockpit/src/lib/hooks/useCashflowLayoutConfig.tsx?

Yes ŌĆö I read it.

### What it does
`useCashflowLayoutConfig`:

- creates a `tableContainerRef`
- uses `ResizeObserver` to measure the container width
- computes how many data columns fit
- clamps the result between:
  - `MIN_COLUMNS = 3`
  - `MAX_COLUMNS = 14`
- falls back to `DEFAULT_COLUMNS = 7` before measurement happens

### Key behavior
- On first render, `columnCount` is `7`
- Once the container is measured, it becomes:

```ts
Math.floor((containerWidth - labelColumnWidth) / dataColumnWidth)
```

then clamped to `[3, 14]`

### One small concern
The effect depends on `[tableContainerRef]`, but that ref object is stable, so the effect will effectively run once. ThatŌĆÖs fine.

However, thereŌĆÖs one subtle issue:

- if `tableContainerRef.current` is still `null` on the first effect run, the hook returns early
- and it **wonŌĆÖt re-run when the DOM node later attaches**, because the ref object itself doesnŌĆÖt change

So in React, this means the `ResizeObserver` may never attach unless the ref is already set by the time the effect runs.

### Possible fix
Use a callback ref or observe in an effect that reacts after the element is mounted.

If you want, I can show you the exact fix in this file.
Can you find references of useCashflowLayoutConfig
LSP server for typescriptreact is still starting up. Try again in a moment.

Yes ŌĆö I found the references.

### References of `useCashflowLayoutConfig`
1. `provider-bw/libs/bw-react-cockpit/src/lib/CashFlowTab.tsx`
   - import at line 21
   - call at line 46

2. `provider-bw/libs/bw-react-cockpit/src/lib/hooks/index.ts`
   - re-export at line 1

### Declaration
- `provider-bw/libs/bw-react-cockpit/src/lib/hooks/useCashflowLayoutConfig.tsx`

### Note
I also tried LSP references, but the TS/TSX server was still starting, so the shell search was the useful result here.

If you want, I can also open `CashFlowTab.tsx` and trace how the hook is being used.
Can you find references of setContainerWidth
LSP server for typescriptreact is still starting up. Try again in a moment.

Yes ŌĆö `setContainerWidth` only appears in:

`provider-bw/libs/bw-react-cockpit/src/lib/hooks/useCashflowLayoutConfig.tsx`

### References
- line 28: state setter declaration
- line 36: called inside `ResizeObserver`

The LSP server still hadnŌĆÖt finished starting, so the shell search is the confirmed result.

If you want, I can also inspect whether this hook is actually measuring a node that is ever mounted, since there may be a lifecycle issue there.

My LSP plugin for Pi seems to be failing. It is currenlty set to typescript-language-server, however...when it tries to do lsp_references. It shows no references for sometihgn that definetly has references.

find: paths must precede expression: `tsconfig.node.json'
find: possible unquoted pattern after predicate `-name'?

Yes ŌĆö and the most likely cause is **not** that `typescript-language-server` is broken, but that the LSP tool is getting the **wrong file context / wrong symbol position / incomplete server setup**.

## What I see in your code
`useCashflowLayoutConfig` is referenced from:

- `provider-bw/libs/bw-react-cockpit/src/lib/CashFlowTab.tsx`
- `provider-bw/libs/bw-react-cockpit/src/lib/hooks/index.ts`

That means references **definitely exist**.

## Why `lsp_references` may return none
Common reasons in Pi LSP setups:

### 1) LSP server not fully initialized for the workspace
If the TS server hasnŌĆÖt properly started with the workspace root, `references` can return incomplete or empty results.

### 2) Wrong cursor position
`lsp_references` depends on the exact symbol under the cursor. If the position is off by even a bit, it may resolve to the wrong token or no symbol at all.

### 3) File not opened/synced in the LSP session
Some Pi LSP setups only track files that have been read/edited in the current session. If the target file or reference files were never synced, results can be poor.

### 4) Multiple tsconfig roots / monorepo structure
Your repo is an Nx monorepo. `typescript-language-server` can be flaky if it doesnŌĆÖt pick the correct project root or TS project boundary.

### 5) The extension is using tree-sitter fallback
Some Pi LSP extensions fall back to tree-sitter when no server is active. Tree-sitter symbol resolution is useful, but references can be weaker than a real TS server.

---

## What I would try first

### A. Confirm the language server starts in the right workspace
Your repo has multiple tsconfig files and nested projects. For TS/TSX, the server should be started from the repo root:

`/home/allank/Documents/work/fidoo-fx-fe`

### B. Open/sync both files before querying references
Make sure Pi has seen:
- `CashFlowTab.tsx`
- `useCashflowLayoutConfig.tsx`

### C. Try `lsp_definition` first
If definition works but references donŌĆÖt, that points to project indexing or workspace symbol issues.

### D. Check whether the LSP plugin is using the correct root
With Nx, a bad root can make TS think the symbol is local-only.

---

## One thing IŌĆÖd definitely do
Your `find` command failed because of quoting. Use:

```bash
find /home/allank/Documents/work/fidoo-fx-fe \( -name 'tsconfig.json' -o -name 'tsconfig.base.json' -o -name 'tsconfig.*.json' \) | sort
```

Or better, since you want `fd`:

```bash
fd -a -g 'tsconfig*.json' /home/allank/Documents/work/fidoo-fx-fe
```

---

## Likely diagnosis
Given your symptom, IŌĆÖd bet on one of these:

1. **LSP started in the wrong project root**
2. **the file wasnŌĆÖt synced/opened when references were queried**
3. **the pluginŌĆÖs references feature is weaker than its definition feature**
4. **the cursor position used for `lsp_references` was wrong**

---

## My recommendation
If you want reliable code navigation in Pi, use the LSP plugin, but:
- verify it starts at the repo root
- prefer `definition`/`references` only after the file has been read
- keep `rg`/`fd` as fallback, not primary

If you want, I can help you **debug the plugin setup step by step** and figure out whether:
- the TS server is starting,
- the workspace root is correct,
- or the plugin is simply not attaching to the right symbol.
Can you try and help me debug the issue?

/home/allank/Documents/work/fidoo-fx-fe/cicd/merge-train/tsconfig.json
/home/allank/Documents/work/fidoo-fx-fe/libs/fnapp-approver/tsconfig.json
/home/allank/Documents/work/fidoo-fx-fe/libs/fnapp-blob-storage/tsconfig.json

## Most likely root cause
Your Pi LSP extension is configured to use `typescript-language-server`, but on your system:

```bash
which typescript-language-server
```

returns nothing.

So the server is **not installed globally / not on PATH**.

That means one of these is happening:
- the extension is failing to start the TS server and falling back
- or it only works when launched through `npx`
- or itŌĆÖs silently not getting a real TS LSP session

That would absolutely explain why `lsp_references` is unreliable or empty.

```

So the extension is enabled.

---

## Biggest red flag
This command failed:

```bash
which typescript-language-server
```

So if the extension expects:

```bash
typescript-language-server --stdio
```

it will fail unless it explicitly uses `npx`.

---

## What I recommend
### Option A ŌĆö install TS LSP globally
Best first step:

```bash
npm i -g typescript-language-server typescript
```

Then verify:

```bash
which typescript-language-server
typescript-language-server --version
```

### Option B ŌĆö configure the Pi LSP extension explicitly
If the extension supports custom server config, point TypeScript to:

```json
{
  "servers": {
    "typescript": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "typescript-language-server", "--stdio"]
    }
  }
}
```

in a project `.pi-lsp.json`.

That avoids depending on a global install.
